data.title: Weak Jobs And Rising Inflation Fuel Stagflation Fears
data.description:
The ADP report and the ISM services reports were both shockingly weaker than expected. This came as a big surprise compared to what analysts had been expecting and will put some extra weight on the job report on Friday.

The ADP private payroll rose by only 37,000 in May, missing estimates of 110,000 and marking the weakest increase since March 2023. Meanwhile, the ISM services report came in at 49.9 versus forecasts for 52.0. Additionally, the new order index fell to 46.4 from 52.3 in April, while the price paid index rose to 68.7 from 65.1. The ISM services report notes that a 49.9 on the overall index corresponds to a 0.4% real GDP growth rate.

The data today was just not good, and again speaks of inflationary pressures, a weaker job market, and slower economic growth, which is a bit of a concern because it points to a stagflation-like environment developing in the economy. While it has not yet shown up in all the hard data, it seems to be a warning sign that higher inflation rates and slower job growth could be on the way.

Higher Inflation Rate

Historically, the services price paid index has tended to be a leading indicator of the Consumer Price Index (CPI). It would seem to suggest that the odds of inflation continuing to decline on a year-over-year basis have run their course and will likely begin to rise. While it is difficult to determine precisely what the inflation rate would look like, it would likely be closer to the upper end of the historical range for inflation, perhaps closer to 4% than the current 2.3%.

Current CPI swap prices suggest that the inflation rate is expected to rise back to 3.3%, and the recent prices paid data indicate that inflation appears to be well on its way to those higher inflation rates.

Weaker Job Growth

While the ADP report doesn't necessarily correlate with the non-farm payroll report due on Friday, it does suggest a slowdown in hiring in May. Whether that shows up in this month's non-farm payroll report is another question, just because the ADP data and the NFP can vary, sometimes significantly. As of now, Kalshi is pricing in 139,000 jobs to be created in May, which is down from around 150,000 at the end of May. Currently, analysts estimate that 130,000 were created in May.

Given this, it seems that more emphasis will be placed on this job report. While one month won't make a trend, it could cause some anxiety across the markets should it come in weaker than expected. The good news, of course, is that historically, at least on the first report, the Non-Farm payroll tends to beat the median estimates.

Certainly, a weaker-than-expected job report could result in interest rates across the Treasury curve falling, the dollar weakening, and, all things being equal, taking some froth off the recent rally in the stock market. But more importantly, it could reignite fears of slower growth, a weakening labor market, and higher inflation, which sounds a lot like stagflation, even if it isn't yet reflected in all the numbers.

data.title: Trump, Powell And Rates: Tariffs On, Tariffs Off
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan & Samuel Rines

Obviously, headlines are swirling at a rather quick pace, so it is important to keep the lines of communication open. This blog is an updated installment to our series we have been publishing since the beginning of the year. Here are our latest thoughts following last week’s whirlwind news:

Trump

  • “Tariffs on, Tariffs off” and repeat. The past couple of weeks have seen a China “deal” coming out of meetings in Switzerland. And then it was EU tariffs being increased. Then it was EU tariffs off. Then it was China not playing by the rules. This is the tariff volatility to expect approaching the early July deadlines.
  • The One Big Beautiful Bill made its way through Congress, only to find significant opposition in the Senate. Something will get done over the summer, but the Senate is going to have their say in the meantime. The State and Local Tax deduction (SALT) is one of the more important consumer stimulus aspects of the bill, and the cap is currently set at $40,000. That is what to watch for whether or not the bill will be a tax cut for consumers into 2026.
  • Consumers have not pulled back—yet. While many of the surveys point to a deterioration in sentiment, the consumer has said one thing and done another. Earnings from companies ranging from Costco (COST) to Ulta (ULTA) have shown resilience, even strength. While not evenly spread across retailers, the impact of tariffs on U.S. consumer spending have not shown up in the results.
  • Looking forward, the volatility of tariffs and the uncertainty around the final language in the tax bill will dominate the headlines and markets. But these uncertainties are likely to be resolved in the coming months (the 90-day deadline for the tariff delay and the summer for the tax bill). Investors would be wise to look through the noise to the likely outcomes and prepare accordingly.

Powell

  • Fed Chair Powell met with President Trump in person for the first time since 2019. While reports indicate the President told Powell the Fed is making a mistake by not cutting rates, the Chair stuck with his messaging that future monetary policy decision-making will be strictly data-dependent.
  • Along those lines, recent Fed-speak has centered on the premise that the FOMC will not have enough information for a potential policy decision until September, at the earliest.
  • The June 18 Fed meeting will contain the Summary of Economic Projections, which includes the dot plot. It’s a close call whether the current median Fed Funds forecast remains at two rate cuts or is downshifted to perhaps only one.
  • The voting members will more than likely emphasize heightened uncertainty to the outlook with risks for higher unemployment and higher inflation both at play.
  • In our opinion, our baseline of one or two rate cuts for this year remains the reasonable case scenario.

Rates

  • After the initial selling, especially in longer-dated maturities, following Moody’s downgrade and the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill, Treasury (UST) market trading has settled down a bit with 10 and 30-year yields now a few basis points lower than two weeks ago.
  • We feel concerns of Selling America are overblown, as last week’s slate of UST fixed and floating rate coupon auctions saw relatively solid domestic and foreign demand. Anxieties following the recent weak showing at the 20-year bond auction failed to take into consideration that this maturity has essentially been investors’ least favorite UST security since its inception.

Conclusion

Our advice remains: stay tuned and don’t get too caught up in the day-to-day headlines because this is going to be a very fluid situation, with lots of volatility.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book "After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy".

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Fixed Income Strategy

As part of WisdomTree's Investment Strategy group, Kevin serves as Head of Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he contributes to the asset allocation team, writes fixed income-related content and travels with the sales team, conducting client-facing meetings and providing expertise on WisdomTree's existing and future bond ETFs. In addition, Kevin works closely with the fixed income team. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management's monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm's Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University's Lubin Graduate School of Business, and a B.S in Finance from Fairfield University.

Original Post

data.title: AXIS Capital And 7% Yield From Its Preferred Stock
data.description:
In today's article, we will examine the undervalued preferred shares of AXIS Capital Holdings Limited (NYSE:AXS). We will analyze their yield spread, which has widened significantly compared to similar instruments, and whether it represents a good buying opportunity for yield-seeking investors.

Company Overview

AXIS Capital Holdings Limited has around $32 billion of total assets and $8 billion of current market capitalization. The company was founded in 2002, and it is headquartered in Pembroke, Bermuda.

Market performance vs. SPY

10 years:

5 years:

Credit Rating

AXS is rated with an A+ credit rating from S&P and with an A from A.M. Best Rating.

Below, you can see the historical total returns of the company. The 10-year total return of AXS is 8.18%.

Preferred stock

AXS has one preferred stock:

(AXS.PR.E) is trading at a price of $20.14 at the moment. Its current yield is 6.85%. The stock pays 5.50% non-cumulative distributions per annum in the form of $0.34375 quarterly ($1.375 annual). It is callable anytime - the call date was on 11/07/2021. AXIS issued 22 million shares of AXS.PR.E on 10/31/2016. The preferred stock is investment grade and has a credit rating from Moody's: Baa3 and from S&P: BBB.

Comparisons with peers

IPO spreads

The first comparison is by IPO spread. We selected such shares that were issued at the time when the AXS.PR.E was issued. We see that here the spread was quite narrow compared to the others:

Spreads now

If we look at the current spreads, we will notice that AXS.PR.E has narrowed the gap significantly, but compared to other preferred stocks, its spread still remains wider.

Delta spreads

If we look at the change in the spread (delta spread), we will see that (AXS.PR.E) is among the least contracted compared to its peers. Even stocks like (CIM.PR.A) and (SCHW.PR.D), which were issued with a higher coupon, are currently trading at a much narrower spread compared to their IPO price. Meanwhile, stocks like (GUT.PR.C) and (BCV.PR.A), issued at the same time and with an identical coupon, are currently offering significantly lower yields.

Credit rating

Bonds

AXS has four OTC bonds with yields between 4.7% and 6.2%. They all are investment grade, with credit ratings Baa1 and Baa2 from Moody's.

The fifteen-year AXS's bond has a yield spread to the 20-year treasury yield (US20Y) of around 1.23%. Below are bonds with maturities up to 10 years. Below are bonds with maturities up to 10 years. The difference in spreads is almost non-existent. The ask yields on AXS and SCHW bonds are almost identical:

Statistics

Let's also pay attention to the statistical graphs related to the performance of preferred shares.

AXS.PR.E (green line) vs. SCHW.PR.D(red):

We see that they are moving in one line, but at one point AXS.PR.E separates for unknown reasons. It looks like it is underperforming. But if we look at the chart of the total return of the common stock:

We do not believe this underperformance is justified, as both AXS bonds and common stock are in no way inferior to their peers.

Fair value

Both stocks are issued at almost the same spread, but their delta spread is almost 1.22% in favor of AXS.PR.E. SCHW.PR.D is currently trading at a market price of $24.56, with a current yield of 6.07%. To achieve the same yield, AXS.PR.E would need to trade at around $22.70 – about $2 higher than its current price. There is no obvious financial reason for SCHW.PR.D to be overvalued relative to AXS.PR.E, unless we assume that the close relationship between President Donald Trump and Charles Schwab has influenced the market valuation.

Conclusion

The preferred stock AXS.PR.E appears undervalued relative to peers with similar credit ratings and coupon yields. Despite its high credit quality and the solid position of AXS, AXS.PR.E trades at a wider spread and higher current yield than other investment-grade stocks.

data.title: Couchbase: Focus On Healthy ARR Growth In A Tough Environment
data.description:
With the stock market surging on hopes for a U.S.-China trade deal, I remain of the opinion that investors need to focus on orienting their portfolios to value, or at least "growth at a reasonable price" plays. Valuation multiples appear stretched for many large caps amid decelerating growth, leading me to seek value in smaller-cap plays.

Couchbase (NASDAQ:BASE), in my view, is a great and often overlooked stock in this regard. The company just reported strong Q1 results, though the stock pulled back modestly on what the market considered to be a lackluster guidance update. Couchbase stock is still up ~15% since the start of the year, but it's down ~10% over the past year - creating a well-timed rebound buying opportunity.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Couchbase in March, when the stock was still trading under $18 per share. Since then, the company has continued to deliver outsized growth in ARR, showcasing stabilization in consumption trends among the company's larger enterprise clients - despite a tough macroeconomy. And while Couchbase still isn't profitable, it is now within spitting distance of breakeven from a pro forma standpoint. With all of this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy rating on the company.

To me, the biggest draw for Couchbase is its value orientation, so let's start with a discussion of the company's latest multiples. At current share post-earnings share prices near $18, Couchbase trades at a market cap of $980 million. After we net off the $142 million of cash (against zero debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, Couchbase's resulting enterprise value is $838 million.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY26 (the year for Couchbase ending in April 2026), the company has guided to $228.3-$232.3 million in revenue - flat at its $230 million midpoint to its prior guidance, which was why the Street was underwhelmed despite Couchbase's Q1 revenue beat. At the same time, however, we note that the company did increase its full-year ARR range to $279.2-$284.3 million, which is 2% higher at the midpoint versus a prior $276.1 million guide.

Couchbase's valuation sits at just 3.6x EV/FY25 revenue. When we compare its valuation against a number of other software stocks that are currently commanding a high single digit or ~10% growth range, we find that Couchbase sits as a very cheap outlier:

In my view, there's no reason Couchbase should sit at such a sharp discount: especially when we consider the fact that the company's ARR is growing at a much more robust ~20% pace. Alongside reliable margin expansion, and an attractive product that should benefit from secular tailwinds as AI investments demand complex big data management strategies, Couchbase is well-positioned for a rebound. Stay long here and use the post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Couchbase's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Couchbase’s revenue grew 10% y/y to $56.5 million, edging out over Wall Street’s expectations of $55.6 million (8% y/y) by a two point margin. Notably as well, Couchbase’s revenue growth didn’t decelerate at all from Q4’s 10% growth pace. Despite the common notion that Couchbase is a tech company that is struggling with lower growth, the fact that it can manage to maintain even growth amid a very uneven macroeconomic environment in which many companies have reported more severe deceleration is notable.

While Couchbase’s revenue growth is decent, its ARR growth is quite impressive, at least relative to revenue. Couchbase added $14.2 million of net new ARR in the quarter to end at $252.1 million in ARR, growing 21% y/y - that is, at double the clip of its revenue growth. The company has been successful at selling deployments of its fully-managed Capella service.

It's notable that amid a macro situation in which many consumption-based software companies have noted budget optimization and weakening usage trends, Couchbase has noted that it has seen strong attach rates and strong customer data ingestion on Capella. Per CEO Matt Cain's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Our pipeline of strategic opportunities where we have the potential to be a true platform winner, continues to grow, which, in combination with our strong Q1 results, reinforces my confidence in our strategy and our ability to maintain our momentum this fiscal year and beyond.

Complementing the strong renewal and expansion activity in Q1, momentum continued with Capella with AR increasing over 80% year-over-year. In addition to new logo wins and migration activity, Capella saw very strong credit consumption growth, driven by customers both moving applications into production as well as launching new ones.

As our Capella base grows, we believe favorable consumption dynamics can continue as both existing and new customers realize our platform's unique performance and scale. Increased consumption across our customer base is indicative of the value we bring and gives us confidence in our ability to deliver sustainable growth.

New products are also helping to contribute to ARR upside. The company recently launched a new offering called Couchbase Edge Server, which it described as a “lightweight” database server that is designed to provide quick, low latency access to critical data to run specific applications that rely on fast-acting queries.

On the probability front, Couchbase continues to march toward breakeven. Pro forma operating margins fell 110bps y/y to 88.7%, from 89.9% in the year-ago quarter, a function of the company’s revenue mix shift toward its managed solutions. We note that Couchbase has best in class gross margins that skew on the high side of even software rivals - where typical enterprise software companies have gross margin profiles in the mid to high 70s.

Meanwhile, the company has managed to offset gross margin downside via disciplined opex. Total opex was flat y/y at $68.5 million, allowing the company to capture economies of scale and lift pro forma operating margins by 6 points y/y to -7%.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, we should be wary of the risks that Couchbase faces. Due to its dependence on customer usage for its revenue, it could see a pullback in revenue growth if customers eventually decide to start optimizing their usage - larger companies ranging from MongoDB (MDB) to Snowflake (SNOW) have been caught in this unfortunate situation. The company also faces competition, particularly from the best-known noSQL database company in the sector, MongoDB.

That being said, I continue to view Couchbase as a company whose strengths are widely overlooked. The fact that the company is growing ARR at a >20% y/y clip, and that ARR exceeds its revenue guidance for the year, is a likely indicator that Couchbase is due for a nice series of beat-and-raise quarters this year.

Stay long and hold out for a fuller rebound here.

data.title: Navitas Semiconductor: After Nvidia's Kiss, It's Now Fairly Valued
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS) is a small-cap power semiconductor firm, which "offers gallium nitride power integrated circuits, silicon carbide power devices, silicon system controllers, and digital isolators for power conversion and charging," according to Seeking Alpha's description. Since the beginning of this year, NVTS stock price first fell by 50% by late April, and then skyrocketed overnight, fueled by a partnership with Nvidia (NVDA). Right now, NVTS is up over 66% on a YTD basis:

I think this YTD price action - the overall strength in NVTS stock that we see over the past few trading sessions - has created a major discrepancy between the stock price and the firm's underlying fundamentals that should theoretically justify the move up, but they fail to do so just yet, in my opinion. I think NVTS's valuation has become too fragile to sustain in the medium term, but I admit that the deal with Nvidia might make Navitas close to being fairly valued. Hence, my "Hold" rating initiation today.

Why Do I Think So?

Navitas Semiconductor's Q1 revenue came in at just $14 million (-39.5% YoY), beating the consensus by only ~$13.2 thousand (or by 0.09%), while the bottom line - their negative EPS of -$0.06 - was in line with the Street's expectations for the period. The general slowdown in channel inventory in EV, solar, and across the industry pressed the top line materially as the management repeatedly mentioned "soft demand" and "inventory correction" throughout the whole earnings call commentary.

As expected, the sequential decline was due to seasonality and soft demand with associated remaining inventory correction. <...>

Moving on to guidance for the first quarter, we currently expect revenues in the range of $14 million to $15 million, reflecting continued softness and industry-wide inventory corrections in solar, EV, and industrial end markets.

Source: Navitas' Q1 earnings call, composed by the author.

The CEO said that the firm needs a quarter or two to see the overhang of silicon carbide inventory gone, then theoretically, the top-line growth should reignite, which I'd perceive lie something uncertain, out of Navitas' control. While Navitas does have a pipeline of ~$450 million worth of design wins, as the management noted itself, the vast majority of that pipeline should come next year, with some trickling in late 2025 and a bit in 2027, so I think it'd be reasonable to spread out this lucrative figure to 2-3 years at least.

On May 21st, 2025, the news broke that Nvidia is going to collaborate with Navitas, taking their GaN and SiC expertise to Nvidia's next-generation 800V HVDC architecture for powering future GPU platforms like Rubin Ultra. And that news hasn't been reflected in Navitas Semiconductor's forward revenues just yet, based on the recent quarterly revisions:

On the other hand, the lack of upward adjustments for the firm's top-line doesn't mean that once such adjustments come, the stock should keep rising - the last week's price action seems to account for prospective reversal in Navitas' business:

Also, Navitas's management consistently mentioned "significant revenue from new design wins" for FY2026 during the Q1 call, so they probably meant Nvidia's contribution to the top line as well.

Anyway, as we know, Rubin Ultra server racks don't come out until mid-2027, so I'd not expect any substantial revenues for Navitas as monetization will likely only begin to be accounted for around that timeframe. Now let's assume that the recent trend in reducing OPEX-to-sales continues and the firm's backlog triples in size, which would effectively lead to a breakeven on a non-GAAP basis by FY2026, and then a doubling of the EPS figure in FY2027. I think that this assumption would fully account for the new partnership with Nvidia.

Consensus Revenue My Revenue Assumption Consensus EPS My EPS Assumption
2025 $64.02 $96.03 -$0.19 -$0.17
2026 $96.42 $289.26 -$0.1 $0.1
2027 $129.82 $389.46 -$0.04 $0.2
2028 NA $467.352 NA $0.4

Source: The author's calculations.

Considering my EPS projection for FY2026 and FY2027, the stock is currently trading at 61.7x and 31x forward price-to-earnings multiples, respectively. The FY2027 P/E multiple would be down to 15.4x if NVTS manages to double its EPS somehow. Money-making firms' P/Es from this industry are ~54x (TTM), on average, based on Damodaran's table, so NVTS would be only slightly overvalued compared to peers in this case. Looking 2 years ahead (at the FY2027 prospective EPS), which is hard to predict right now, but when following my assumptions, the stock would become truly undervalued.

The Bottom Line

I should note that initially, I wanted to rate Navitas Semiconductor stock as a "Sell," but then I realized that the newly announced deal with Nvidia may indeed add a lot of value - the backlog as of Q1 (at $450 million) was giving too low of a comparison base for their future top line figure, so it can easily double in the next 2-3 years from now as Nvidia prepares its Rubin Ultra server racks shipments. Based on my math and assumptions, which may be quite arguable in nature for some investors, the stock is close to being fairly valued now, when we look out 2-3 years from now.

Anyway, the lack of earnings visibility and the current challenges on EV and other end markets stop me from issuing a "Buy" - NVTS is a "Hold" for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Walmart: Some Ways We Could Profit Between Earnings
data.description:
Article Thesis

Walmart (NYSE:WMT) is an intriguing case. On one side, we have a relatively clear overvaluation, for example with a PEG ratio of 4.89, compared to 3.63 five-years average. More about valuation later.

On the other side, there is a curious bullishness of Wall St. analysts:

We can see a much more balanced view from SA analysts, tilted towards bearishness:

Although Wall St. analysts are many times driven by other things than the fair value of a stock, and many times they lag the stock movements, the problem is that they can influence the short-term price. Long-term, stocks almost always find their ways towards their fair value. Of course, a fair value is just an approximation, but I would say that there is not a high uncertainty for a business like Walmart.

It is, therefore, a difficult decision to short the stock when Wall St. analysts are bullish and very bullish. The potential profit from a short sale is also not very appealing (more about valuation later). But if we look at the options market, we can instantly find a plethora of…options, for example short-term strategies used between (or before) earnings, which can eventually be transformed into other longer-term strategies.

Business Analysis and Economic Moat

Walmart is the biggest retailer in the U.S., but they actually have three business segments:

Walmart U.S.: with stores in all states and e-commerce, this segment represents 69% of total sales.

This segment has an economic moat based on cost benefits. With its very large scale, Walmart has negotiating power, and a large distribution network. Normally I would say this is a strong economic moat, but this is eroding today due to the tariffs that Walmart “should eat”, even if more than two-thirds of what they sell in the U.S. is made, assembled or grown locally. The company expressed concerns about these tariffs during their last earnings call:

The immediate challenge is obviously navigating the impact of tariffs here in the U.S. Our mindset and approach haven't changed since our investor conference last month in Dallas. I want to thank President Trump and Secretary Bessent for the progress made recently. We're hopeful that it leads to a longer term agreement between the U.S. and China that would result in even lower tariffs. We will do our best to keep our prices as low as possible. But given the magnitude of the tariffs, even at the reduced levels announced this week, we aren't able to absorb all the pressure given the reality of narrow retail margins.

Walmart International: with stores in 18 countries and e-commerce, this segment represents 18% of total sales, China standing out with a double-digit growth last year.

This segment might have the same economic moat based on cost benefits, but with much smaller markets this is from the beginning only a narrow economic moat.

Sam’s Club: a membership-only U.S. club, representing 13% of total sales.

Like any network, this segment develops a moat based on…network effects: more offers can attract more customers, however these offers come with free shipping and discounts, so I would say only a narrow economic moat.

Overall, I would say somewhere between a narrow and a strong economic moat, trending down. This is also reflected in their ROI (Return On Investment) in excess of WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital):

However, please be aware that they are using Operating Income adding back Depreciation & Amortization. If we use NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax), a more frequently used metric, this 15.5% would translate to about 11%, still above WACC, but not by much (more about WACC later), stressing again an economic moat, but not that strong.

Financial Analysis

In the last quarter, Walmart U.S. comp grew by 4.5%, while international sales grew by 7.8%, driven by China and Flipkart. Sam's U.S. comp grew by 6.7%, excluding fuel. eCommerce has a high contribution with global growth of 22%.

That’s why the expected growth rate of 4%-5% for the next two to three years is plausible:

Switching to profitability, the long-term trend is for higher growth than for sales:

There is nothing about this current period that makes us feel differently about anything we've previously said about our long-term financial framework to grow annual sales about 4% and operating income faster than sales.

This is driven by higher margin businesses like membership and advertising. eCommerce also crossed the profitability threshold, and I expect it to also help drive margins higher, since eCommerce is naturally a more scalable business segment.

However, there is, of course, the uncertainty brought by tariffs, a restoration of dramatically higher tariff levels could even jeopardize their ability to grow earnings YoY.

Finally, Walmart has a strong financial position, with an AA credit rating from Fitch, reflecting its dominant retail position and healthy leverage, and the same from S&P Global.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, we can first look at Walmart’s five-year averages, because Walmart is not a cyclical stock, and today's multiples are about 40% above:

Then, compared to peers, we can observe about the same difference, between 30% and 50% above:

For an absolute valuation, I built a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) with growth in perpetuity combined with EV/EBITDA exit. My assumptions are:

  • Revenue growth: starting from consensus for this year (see above section), I model 4.5% growth for 3 years, then 4% growth for another 3 years and finally 3.5% growth another 3 years, driven by eCommerce and emerging markets.
  • EBIT margin constant this year at 4.3% due to tariff pressures, then slightly growing towards 5.5% in 2034 driven by higher margin segments (see above section).
  • Growth in perpetuity: 3%, in line with global GDP growth.
  • Terminal EV/EBITDA: 13x, close to Walmarts’s 5Y average.
  • Tax rate: 22% (from ~23.5% in 2024 and declining).
  • CapEx: between 3% and 3.5% of sales, according to the company’s guidance; I use 3.5% for this year, then decreasing to 3% in 2028.
  • WACC = 7.6%: Cost of Equity = 8% (4.25% Risk-free rate, 5% equity-risk and 0.75 re-levered Beta), with ~90% weight, and after-tax Cost of Debt = 4%, with ~10% weight.

Fair Value is $68.2, about 45% below the current price (~$99.5), which is backed by the relative valuation. Or that means about 31% potential downside from the current price to Fair Value. We can further run a sensitivity analysis, by varying WACC and growth in perpetuity:

Fair Value ($)

WACC=7%

WACC=7.6%

WACC=8.2%

Growth=2%

69.6

64

59.2

Growth=3%

75.35

68.2

62.4

Growth=4%

84.9

74.8

67.2

We can observe that, even with more optimistic assumptions like WACC = 7% and growth in perpetuity = 4%, the stock still seems slightly overvalued with a Fair Value of $84.9.

Some Strategies Between Earnings

Setup

We have a company with narrow to strong economic moat, but with slow growth and about 31% potential downside to Fair Value. If we had 30% growth I could understand, but in this case, I see no justification for this valuation.

That does not mean that the stock cannot stay overvalued another year or two. For example, lower than 30% tariffs for China can be short-term positive for the stock. Or another sector rotation due to macro fears from sensitive to defensive industries can keep the stock higher short-term without any merit. Besides, Wall St. analysts are, curiously, mostly bullish. So, I am not interested in shorting the stock.

However, focusing on the power of options, we can see another setup: there are more than two and a half months until earnings (August 21), which means we can sell an option expiring just before earnings, on August 15, because options expiring before earnings lose value very quickly. At the same time, we can buy an option expiring after earnings (about one month after earnings, on September 19), with the idea to sell it together with the other option, because the next month option should normally gain some value just before earnings due to higher IV (Implied Volatility).

Strategy #1

One way to implement this setup is through a Calendar Spread (it does not matter very much if Call or Put options) and, since we are more or less bearish, we should place the strike price below the current price, for example at $92.5:

We can see that this strategy could have a remarkable leverage, with ROI about 160% in two and a half months with the stock exactly at $92.5 at expiration, because the short option will expire worthless, while the long one will have plenty of extrinsic value (especially just before earnings).

On the other side, what I don’t like is the very narrow range of profitability: between about $88.5 and $97.5. Outside that range, the long option will have more extrinsic value to lose. That means if the stock is going down very quickly, I should sell it when it hits $92.5 for a minor profit: the more distance to expiration, the lower the profit at $92.5, because the short 92.5 Put Aug 15 will still have extrinsic value.

But what to do if the stock will suddenly jump on its way down from $93 to, say, $87 (due to an unexpected event / announcement, like the reversal of China tariffs to 140%): we would probably need to close the position with a minor loss, despite the fact that I’m counting on the stock going down, since I find the stock overvalued!

Strategy #2

Let’s complicate the strategy just a bit, by choosing different strike prices, which gives us a Diagonal Spread (as usual, for investors not familiar with the strategy I do recommend them to read but to test it first in a demo account for about one year):

We observe that we can collect about 745 – 335 = $410 with the two Call options above.

The cost for this diagonal Call is a bit trickier…let’s suppose that, in a worst-case scenario, the stock will go to $125 on August 15:

  • The short 95 Call Aug 25 will be bought back for $3000 (125 – 95, multiplied by 100), maybe a few dollars more (let’s say $3020), because it’s usually better to buy it back just before expiration. Otherwise, since it is ITM (In-The-Money), we will be assigned (we’ll have short 100 shares), which is not a very big problem because we can close it immediately after assignment; however, if there is an ex-dividend on the same date (it can happen), we’ll need to pay the dividend ($24).
  • The long 105 Call Sep 19 will still have a bit of extrinsic value, and it will value more than $2000, depending on the IV at that time, and I’ve just said that IV should normally go higher before earnings (even for 90 days options); let’s suppose just $70 extrinsic value, so we could sell it for $2070.

Overall, at $125 just before August 25, we could close the position for about 2070 – 3020 = -$950. If we subtract the collected premium of $410, we get a $540 loss, and this is the cost of the strategy.

Some conclusions we can observe from the chart above:

  • Maximum profit with the stock exactly at $95 is about $450, for 450 / 540 = ~83% ROI, because the short 95 Call will expire worthless, while the long 105 Call will have enough extrinsic value. Lower than for the Calendar Spread, but still impressive in two and a half months.
  • The strategy gains at least that collected premium of $410 (or a ROI of 410 / 550 = ~75%) at any low price, since both Calls will be far OTM (Out-of-The-Money), even with the stock at $68 (Fair Value), or even at…zero, and this is what I like the most compared to the Calendar.
  • On the upside, we can see the break-even at ~$100.4 (a bit higher than the Calendar because we have a short at $95 instead of $92.5).
  • Above $100.4, the strategy will lose at an accelerated pace (just like the Calendar), but…in this case we will be even more bearish. Let’s suppose that Walmart goes to $110 and the strategy will lose about $350; now, we can switch to a more bearish strategy, like an ATM (At-The-Money) long Put Spread carefully chosen so our potential profit will be higher than this loss of $350, for an overall profit. With the stock at $125, we could switch an even more bearish strategy, like a long Put.

Strategy #3

And now, of course, we can combine the two strategies:

As we can see, we'll have higher ROI than for a Diagonal somewhere between $88 and $96, with that pronounced peak at $92.5 brought by the Calendar.

With the stock down, we still have a remarkable profit at any level, albeit a little lower than with the Diagonal, due to the loss brought by the Calendar.

With the stock up, the loss curve is about the same as with the Diagonal, since both the Diagonal and the Calendar erode at about the same rate.

Risks

I’ve just described in detail possible continuations if the strategy will bring us a loss in a first phase, on August 15, or even after two iterations, so we could still end with on overall profit. We might be able to open a second iteration with the stock at ~$110 (the proposed long Put Spread) for an overall profit without needing more funds, but a for a third iteration at ~$125 (the proposed long Put), or even between $110-$125 we will need for sure to add more funds for an overall profit. That’s why I target a smaller initial exposure, maximum ½ position (think that when we buy a stock, we can start with ½ position or less, and then we can average down).

Even more, for the moment I have only the Diagonal and I might also add the Calendar if the price keeps going up, in order to get a lower cost (the Diagonal makes a bit more than 80% of the combined cost, which I want to keep it under 1/2 of a normal position).

Of course, there is also the risk that the stock will go up, so against my position, and will stay up. However, to stay at such elevated levels (it will be significantly overvalued) for years, that means more than short-term market whims. Perhaps zero tariffs for China (an unlikely scenario). Or an even higher operating leverage (remember that I already modeled an increase in EBIT margin from 4.3% to 5.5%) but that would mean a main reorientation of the business model, for example towards mainly online (unlikely again). If there will be some signs of this scenario until August, I will still go with my proposed continuation strategies (or another bearish strategy), but I will target only a tie instead of an overall profit, trying to keep the overall exposure lower (at maximum ½ position).

Takeaway

Walmart has a narrow-to-strong economic moat, based on cost advantages especially from the Walmart U.S. segment, with its very large market share, but also on network effects from its Sam's Club segment.

Growth prospects are steady, with ~4% revenue growth expected, driven by eCommerce and emerging markets. Earnings are expected to grow at a higher pace, based on higher margins segments like membership, ads and, in the future, eCommerce. However, margins will be more or less pressured by tariffs this year.

Derived from a 10-year DCF model, and backed by comparisons with Walmart's 5-year averages or with peers, my Fair Value estimate is $68.2, about 31% potential downside from the current price (~$99.5).

Slightly bearish options strategies like calendar and diagonal spreads (or combinations) offer attractive risk/reward setups pre-earnings. We could sell an option expiring Aug 15, a few days before earnings, and buy an option expiring in September with the intention to also close it before earnings, when there should normally be a higher IV. If the stock will go up and the strategy will have a loss, we could eventually switch to more bearish longer-term strategies, since the stock will be even more overvalued (without major fundamental changes).

data.title: Couchbase Sees Capella Demand But Macro Concerns Continue
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Couchbase (NASDAQ:BASE) recently reported its FQ1 2026 financial results, beating both revenue and earnings estimates.

I previously analyzed BASE in February 2025 with a Hold outlook due to the potential for growth from agentic AI demand.

The company continues to see demand from customers for its Capella platform, but its topline revenue growth rate is slowing, operating losses remain substantial, and its capital allocation performance is shareholder dilutive.

So, my outlook remains a neutral Hold for the near term.

Couchbase’s Market And Approach

BASE’s primary business is in the Database-as-a-Service market via its NoSQL database configuration SaaS platform.

The company has been touting its Capella platform, which seeks to use Agentic AI technologies to assist organizations in automating various functions.

According to a 2024 market research report by MarketsAndMarkets, the global cloud database and DBaaS market was approximately $21 billion in 2023 and was forecasted to reach $57 billion by 2028.

If accomplished, this growth rate would be an impressive CAGR of 22% from 2024 to 2028.

The main drivers for this projected growth include strong growth in demand for greater capabilities and ease-of-use in cloud database systems that can operate in a variety of environments.

Additionally, SMBs will account for increased breadth of demand for these functions.

The chart below shows the forecasted growth trajectory by region through 2028 for the cloud database and DBaaS market:

Industry players include the following companies of varying sizes. Couchbase runs up against strong competition from a wide variety of industry participants with point solutions to bundled offerings:

  • IBM (IBM)
  • Oracle (ORCL)
  • Amazon (AMZN)
  • Google (GOOG)
  • Microsoft (MSFT)
  • SAP SE (SAP)
  • MongoDB (MDB)
  • EnterpriseDB
  • Redis Labs
  • MariaDB
  • Neo4j
  • MemSQL
  • DataStax
  • Others

The company sells its software via a standard 'land and expand' sales and marketing approach as well as through ecosystem partners such as resellers and integrators.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary characteristics:

  • Strengths: Differentiated products, Capella Agentic AI system, Enterprise focus
  • Weaknesses: Lower brand awareness, smaller-sized player
  • Opportunities: AI enhancements, edge computing growth
  • Threats: Intense competition from much larger participants, need for continuous R&D spend to keep up with competition

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen 10% YoY from strong ARR growth. Operating income by quarter (red line) has remained negative but continues to make incremental improvement on lower SG&A costs.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has risen in the last few quarters, although down one percentage point YoY. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have fallen due to an improved focus on strategic accounts. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have been trending lower in recent quarters despite continued investment in its Capella platform.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have made some steady progress toward breakeven, but remain a long way from that important milestone.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP)

For balance sheet results, BASE ended the quarter with $147.2 million in cash, equivalents and short-term investments and no debt.

In the past 12 months, BASE’s stock price has fallen by about 18% vs. that of competitor MongoDB (MDB)’s similar drop of 18%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for Couchbase:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

EV/EBITDA - FWD

Rev. Growth Est. - FWD

FY 2026 EPS Estimate - FWD

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance has produced improvement primarily due to a less negative operating margin offsetting a lower topline revenue growth rate:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

FQ3 2025

FQ1 2026

Revenue Growth %

19.3%

16.4%

Operating Margin

-37.2%

Total

-17.9%

-12.4%

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash used was $18.8 million, and capital expenditures were $3 million.

The company also paid a whopping $61.3 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($81.1 million).

The net dollar increase in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $9.9 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 29.3%, a very high number for a mature company.

Compared to MongoDB’s recent 24.6% result, Couchbase is paying out significantly higher SBC as a percentage of revenue while having a higher CapEx burden and still generating negative free cash flow.

Management hasn’t recently disclosed any future plans for capital allocation in relation to its shares, so investors are in the dark as to this aspect of its financial operations.

Why I’m Neutral On Couchbase

Couchbase has shown a reduced topline revenue growth rate along with improved, though still negative, operating margin.

This has been a common theme among operating loss-making software companies since the sharp increase in the cost of capital has penalized them heavily.

However, they have followed a similar trajectory of essentially sacrificing topline revenue growth for better operating results, putting at risk their ‘growth’ label and valuation in the bargain.

The company’s growth strategy entails investing in its Capella DBaaS platform, both on-platform features and integrations with ecosystem partners, positioning its offerings for higher-value propositions, and expanding its routes to market.

The graphic below illustrates the frequency of various typically negative keywords in the firm’s most recent conference call:

The chart shows the company continues to face macroeconomic challenges as clients take longer to make final purchase decisions, resulting in longer sales cycles.

Couchbase is also facing customer count decline as developers have moved from its starter pack promotion over to its free tier, although management says that really is expanding the top of the funnel.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing BASE at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.7x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 13.1% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 14% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. Couchbase’s NRR of 114%.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.3x on May 30, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, Couchbase is currently being valued by investors at a discount to the broader index as of May 30, 2025.

I’ve also compared Couchbase to competitor MongoDB, with their major metrics shown in the table here:

Metric

MongoDB

Couchbase

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

MDB is growing revenue at a faster growth rate, even though it is a much larger company and has positive EBITDA and free cash flow per share, so I’m not surprised it is garnering a higher valuation.

Also, the market’s EV/Sales multiple has dropped 20% in the past year, suggesting it is enamored with BASE’s growth prospects.

Looking ahead, management raised full-year guidance for total revenue and ARR and said it is aiming for profitability in fiscal 2027. If so, it has a long way to go to achieve GAAP operating breakeven, even with continued uptake on its Capella platform.

While operating losses are narrowing, they remain elevated. Given the company’s dilutive capital allocation, lack of visibility on capital needs, and a decelerating topline growth rate, I’m a neutral Hold on Couchbase for the near term.

data.title: CorMedix: Exceeding Expectations
data.description:
Today, I am putting CorMedix Inc. (NASDAQ:CRMD) in the spotlight for the second time here in 2025. When I first covered this small cap name, CorMedix was just entering full blown commercialization with its flagship product, an antimicrobial catheter lock solution called DefenCath. I noted this device has the potential to reduce catheter-related bloodstream infections in adult patients with kidney failure and become a 'best of breed' product in this niche market. Based on the numbers the company has put up since that article in the first week in January, both CorMedix and DefenCath have substantially exceeded initial expectations. As the result the stock has had a nice run here in 2025. Can the rally continue? An updated analysis follows below.

CorMedix, Inc. is headquartered in New Jersey. The company is focused on developing treatments for and devices to prevent infectious and inflammatory diseases. As noted before, the company's primary asset is DefenCath which comes in a vial of a patented blend of taurolidine, heparin, and citrate. This combination has compared favorably against heparin-based catheter lock solutions in reducing catheter-related bloodstream infections or CRBSIs. The product was fully approved just over a year ago. CorMedix's stock currently trades around $12.75 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $860 million.

DefenCath primary markets are the outpatient dialysis segment, which accounts for the lion's share of sales, as well as hospital inpatient settings. The product is the only FDA approved catheter lock solution that delivers significant risk reduction in CRBSIs.

The company is looking to expand the indications DefenCath is approved for. CorMedix recently initiated a Phase 3 study for the reduction of Central Line Associated Blood Stream Infections or CLABSIs for individuals receiving Total Parenteral Nutrition. Management is aiming for a NDA for this indication in late 2026 or early 2027. They believe this could add $150 million to $200 million annually to their potential market.

Recent Results:

The company posted Q1 numbers on May 6th. The firm delivered GAAP earnings of 30 cents a share, six cents a share above the consensus. CorMedix produced net income of $20.6 million and adjusted EBITDA of $23.6 million. Management did a good job of controlling expenses given the sales ramp up. Operating expenses rose 9% year-over-year to $17.4 million. Full year operating expenses are expected to come in between $72 million to $78 million in FY2025.

Revenues came in at $39.1 million, besting expectations by some $3 million. This compares to negligible sales in the same period a year ago and sequentially up from $31.2 million of revenues in Q4. Outpatient dialysis continues to account to the vast majority of sales. However, shipments to inpatient facilities more than doubled from a year ago to make up six percent of overall revenues. In addition, CorMedix's inpatient sales team is now fully staffed, trained and out in the field.

Management also noted that it now believes first half net sales will come in at the top end of its previous guidance of $62 million to $70 million. Leadership based this upwardly revised view on the latest order trends and inventory tracking with current purchasing customers. It also believes Q2 sales will come in near the top end of their previous guidance of $23 million to $31 million. Overall FY2025 sales will be impacted by the timing and scale of uptake of a large dialysis operator customer CorMedix recently signed.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

CorMedix is view positively by the analyst community. Since first quarter results hit the wires, four analyst firms including RBC Capital and Needham have reiterated Buy ratings on the stock. All four ratings contained minor upward price target revisions. Price targets range from $13 to $20 a share.

Just over 12% of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. There has been no insider activity in the stock so far in 2025. The company ended Q1 with $77.5 million in cash and marketable securities on the balance sheet after producing $19.7 million in operating cash flow during the quarter. CorMedix listed no long-term debt on the 10-K filed for Q1.

Conclusion:

CorMedix lost 30 cents a share (GAAP) on $43.5 million in revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has CorMedix with 80 cents a share of profits in FY2025 on $144 million in sales. They project $1.51 a share of earnings in FY2026 on revenue growth of just over 50%.

That leaves the stock trading at roughly 16 times forward earnings and just over eight times FY2026E EPS. A bit cheaper if net cash on the balance sheet is taken into consideration. More than reasonable valuations even after a 55% gain so far in 2025.

The major risk around CorMedix is the company's complete reliance on DefenCath. If the product has any sort of issues in the future (Ex, reimbursement changes), it would be an existential risk to the company. While unlikely, that needs to be acknowledged. DefenCath does have 10 years of market exclusivity from its FDA approval it should be noted.

I have accumulated a fairly large stake in CRMD this year on the increasingly positive news flow. I have done so via covered call orders as liquidity in the options against this equity is solid and the option premiums are attractive. I will be adding to this stake as my previous covered call positions expire in the money in the months ahead. I will look for any decent dip in the stock to do so as CRMD seems like a solid long-term 'Accumulate' given the company's growth trajectory and current valuations.

data.title: Entravision Stock: Well-Positioned As Adtech Business Grows, And As FCC Eyes Deregulation
data.description:
Since the publication of my prior article on Entravision Communications (NYSE:EVC) last November, shares in the advertising technology and broadcasting company have pulled back to prior lows. This comes despite numerous positives for EVC stock emerging since the start of the year.

For one, there has been a key positive that has emerged for Entravision's legacy business. In the near future, the company may have the opportunity to capitalize on this development. Alongside this positive, the latest financials for EVC's newer, faster growing business suggest that this unit is worth materially more than I estimated it was worth in my prior write-up.

Although a breakout moment for EVC may not arrive immediately, as before, time is somewhat on your side. At least, given how Entravision continues to pay a very high dividend, adding a "paid while you wait" factor to this name.

With all of this in mind, let's dive in, take a closer look, and see why EVC remains a great opportunity among microcap value stocks.

EVC Stock: Recap of Recent Performance

Why has Entravision stock performed poorly over the past seven months? Chalk it up to the fading of election-year bullishness, plus the emergence of economic uncertainty in the wake of recent macro developments.

[object HTMLElement]

While not for certain, perhaps investors bid up EVC stock ahead of the 2024 U.S. Presidential Elections, due to how much Entravision benefits from the election cycle, given how it produces a short-term boost in ad demand and prices for the company's portfolio of Spanish-language television and radio stations.

In turn, once election day entered the rearview mirror, focus shifted back to other factors, such as the company's earnings release, interestingly enough held the day after election day. As discussed in my last write-up, Entravision delivered mixed results during Q3 2024, with revenue rising due to election year demand but earnings dropping due to one-time accounting items.

Following the fading of bullish momentum in November, shares were more-or-less rangebound until February, when EVC began to experience sharper price declines again. This is when the aforementioned "economic uncertainty" factor began to have a negative impact on shares.

Concerns about the impact of higher tariffs on the U.S. economy turned into concerns about advertising demand, further souring enthusiasm for EVC stock. However, while sentiment for EVC has continued to lean bearish, this could soon change. At least, assuming that the market begins to appreciate both recent favorable regulatory developments, as well as key takeaways from Entravision's latest quarterly earnings release.

FCC Catalyst Still on the Table

A key positive that has emerged since election day has been FCC leadership changes made by the new Trump administration. I did not touch on this factor in my last EVC stock write-up, but I did discuss it in a write-up on Sinclair (SBGI) back in April.

The FCC, under the leadership of Brendan Carr, has launched a massive deregulation initiative, codenamed "Delete, Delete, Delete." This initiative is viewed to be a prelude to the regulatory agency eliminating broadcast ownership rules regarded as being outdated in the digital era, such as the national TV station ownership cap, as well as caps on ownership of multiple stations in the same market.

If these regulations are eliminated, this in turn could spark the next big wave of media M&A. In this scenario, Entravision would be well-positioned to sell its radio and television stations to a larger competitor, possibly obtaining top dollar for these assets, due to the potential for strategic acquirers to benefit from cost synergies.

Alongside deregulation, the new FCC regime has publicly stated its interest in holding another broadcast spectrum auction. Entravision benefited greatly from the last spectrum auction held in 2017, generating a total of $263.6 million in gross proceeds.

Admittedly, it's unclear how soon, if at all, if the FCC's recent actions and announcements will result in a boon for Entravision. However, as mentioned above, this is not the only positive EVC has going for it.

Latest Earnings Release Provides Other Promising Takeaways

On May 8, Entravision released fiscal results for the quarter ending March 31, 2025. Although the legacy Media segment is mired in a post-election slump, with sales down 10% year-over-year, and operating income falling back into the red, factors such as macro uncertainty don't seem to be affecting EVC's Advertising Technology segment.

Last quarter, this segment reported 57% sales growth, as well as a nearly fourfold increase in operating profit. With this in mind, it's time to reassess how much this business is potentially worth. Later on, I'll provide my estimate for the value of Entravision's Advertising Technology segment.

Once again, one-time writedowns resulted in GAAP losses, but the reason behind these latest non-cash charges could be seen as yet another positive for EVC stock going forward. There were a total of $48.9 million in non-cash charges.

These charges were due primarily to the sale of two TV stations in Mexico, as well as due to Entravision's vacating of its previous corporate headquarters in Santa Monica, California.

Eliminating both non-core assets, as well as continuing to focus on cost-cutting measures, Entravision may be poised to report stronger results in the coming quarters, including perhaps a return to GAAP profitability.

On the Q1 2025 post-earnings conference call, Entravision's management provided further positive takeaways. As CEO Michael Christenson discussed on the call, the company reduced corporate expenses by $4.5 million during Q1 2025, or by around $18 million on an annualized basis.

As noted by CFO Mark Boelke on the call, the company expects operating expenses for the Advertising Technology business to "even out on an annualized basis with revenue increasing faster than expense to generate meaningful operating leverage in our Ad Tech & Services business."

EVC Stock Valuation

At current prices, Entravision Communications has a market cap of $181 million. Add to this figure $235.3 million in debt and lease liabilities, and subtract $78.2 million in cash, and we get an enterprise value of just over $338.1 million.

On a trailing twelve month (TTM) basis, EVC may look pricey, compared to other publicly-traded names in the space. Based on TTM EBITDA, Entravision trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of 11x. This represents a valuation premium to that of peers like Sinclair, Gray Media (GTN), and E.W. Scripps (SSP), all of which trade at TTM EBITDA multiples in the 5x-6x range.

However, beyond the fact that these names are likely overlooked and undervalued as well, remember that Entravision is only partially a broadcasting company. Advertising Technology, made up of the company's Smadex and Adwake subsidiaries, generates a majority of Entravision's overall revenue. Last quarter, this segment also generated nearly 90% of segment-level EBITDA (segment operating profit plus depreciation and amortization).

In past coverage, I have provided a breakup value-based estimate for the underlying value of EVC stock. I will do so again, as seen below:

Media Segment (Entravision's Broadcasting Assets)

Last time we estimated the value of Entravision's broadcasting assets, we based it on the assumption of $28.5 million in annual EBITDA, based upon calculations that incorporated recent quarterly results, as well as estimated how much of Entravision's corporate overhead could be attributed to this segment.

This time, given the near-term slump of EVC's broadcasting business, I will use an alternative valuation, to assess how much these stations could be worth to a strategic buyer, especially if the FCC ultimately rescinds what remains of regulations that limit national and local TV and radio station ownership.

Last quarter, the Media segment reported revenue of just under $41 million. We will annualize this, to $164 million, even as it's possible that this segment reports much higher revenue in subsequent quarters. The EV/Sales ratios of Entravision radio and television broadcasting peers range widely, with names like radio station owner Sinclair trading at an EV/Sales ratio of 1.3, while Gray Media trades at an EV/Sales ratio of around 2.1.

However, erring on the side of conservatism, we will use a 1.5x sales multiple for this business. This provides us with a valuation of around $246 million, not that far off from the $228 million we estimated previously.

Advertising Technology (Smadex, Adwake)

Last quarter, Entravision's advertising technology segment generated $6.5 million in operating income. Add in depreciation, and quarterly EBITDA for this segment was around $7 million. This comes to around $28 million on an annualized basis.

Given management's remarks on the latest conference call, I feel confident to assume that Smadex and Adwake will generate even stronger results in the coming quarters.

Yes, you may be thinking, "what about corporate overhead expenses?" Good question. As before, I'll prorate corporate overhead. Last quarter, this segment represented around 55% of overall revenue. Corporate overhead last quarter also came in at around $7.8 million. With this, we'll shift $4.3 million in quarterly overhead, or around $17.2 million over to Advertising Technology.

Following this adjustment, Adtech is generating about $10.8 million in EBITDA on an annualized basis. Given how quickly this business is growing, I believe it should be valued at the upper-end of valuations of advertising stocks. Other programmatic advertising stocks like PubMatic (PUBM) and TechTarget (TTGT) are trading at EBITDA multiples in the high-teens to low-twenties.

Hence, assigning a 20x EBITDA multiple may be reasonable at this point. At this multiple, the Advertising Technology Segment is worth around $216 million, considerably more than the $137.6 million I estimated previously.

Adding it All Together

Adding these estimated values of the Media and Advertising Technology segments, we get an estimated gross valuation of around $444 million. Subtract $235.3 million in total debt/lease liabilities, and add back $78.2 million in cash, and we get a valuation of $286.9 million, or around $3.15 per share, a nearly 60% premium to the current trading price of EVC stock.

A Caveat and Two Silver Linings

While Entravision arguably trades at a discount to its underlying value, there is a caveat to keep in mind: realizing this value will take time, even as regulatory trends appear favorable. Again, it's unclear how soon the FCC could potentially de-regulate broadcast media, but presumably if it happens, it will happen between now and the next U.S. Presidential election.

In the meantime, if the lagging performance of Entravision's broadcast assets persists, overshadowing the further growth of the Advertising Technology segment, EVC could remain rangebound, or worse, fall again to lower prices.

That said, alongside this caveat are two silver linings. First, my valuation estimates may be conservative. I may be using an aggressive multiple to value the Adtech business based on current results, but if growth continues, this business could end up being worth far more than my $216 million current estimate.

The same goes for broadcasting, albeit not to an extreme of an extent. With Entravision cutting costs, as well as prioritizing content like news, as well as expanding its local digital advertising sales capabilities, the Media segment may be able to become materially more profitable, even in non-election years.

Don't forget, either, that Entravision could benefit from another spectrum auction. Even if not as significant of a windfall as the first spectrum auction, such an event would likely add a noticeable boost to potential upside.

Second, besides the fact I am conservatively-valuing Entravision's businesses, there may be not one, but two natural buyers for EVC's broadcasting stations, just waiting in the wings to scoop up these assets once deregulation happens.

For one, we have TelevisaUnivision, which controls Class U shares that give it a say in whether Entravision can be sold, or if it can sell its Univision and UniMás affiliates.

Although this dynamic suggests that TelevisaUnivision could stymie potential post-deregulation M&A, or even use these shares as leverage to acquire Entravision's stations at a favorable price, given how cheap EVC stock is today, a favorable price could still represent a windfall for the company.

Alongside TelevisaUnivision, there's also Estrella Media. Recently acquired by publicly-traded MediaCo Holding (MDIA), Estrella is another Spanish-language broadcast station owner. As of late, both Estrella and Entravision have been partnering up in various ways. Last fall, the two companies entered an ad-sales partnership for their respective radio station businesses. Moreover, as seen from Entravision's new corporate address: 1 Estrella Way, Burbank, CA.

If this means a merger between Entravision and MediaCo/Estrella is in the cards is anyone's guess. MediaCo has a market cap of just $61.2 million, and likely isn't in the position to snap up EVC. However, maybe this is the prelude to MediaCo/Estrella buying up EVC's radio stations. Or, perhaps the two companies may even be considering further partnerships. These in turn could help boost EVC's profitability and in turn its stock price.

The Bottom Line on EVC Stock

It may be frustrating that Entravision continues to be overlooked and undervalued, but in time, it could pay off in a big way, for those patient enough to hold onto a position.

In the years ahead, regulatory changes could enable EVC to sell off its legacy business at a favorable valuation. Another spectrum windfall may be in the cards as well. During this time frame, the company's Advertising Technology segment could continue growing at a rapid pace, becoming an even more valuable business in the process.

Worth at least $3 per share today, in time these assets could be worth an even greater premium to EVC's current share price. Not only that, as mentioned before, while waiting for Entravision's true value to be realized, investors benefit from EVC's high dividend. Still at 5 cents per share, this quarterly dividend gives the stock a 10.05% forward yield. Recent cost cutting, plus the Adtech segment's move to profitability, may increase the chances that this cash payout stays intact.

Taking all these factors into account, consider EVC stock still worthy of a buy, at present price levels and especially on any near-term weakness.

data.title: Joby's $1 Billion Saudi Deal: A Big Dream With Big Questions
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (NYSE:JOBY) announced just this week, on June 3rd, that they signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Abdul Latif Jameel ("ALJ"), a massive business group in Saudi Arabia, to potentially deliver up to 200 of their electric aircraft. The total value of this partnership could go as far as $1 billion over the next few years. For the company that always brings zeros in sales, this is incredible, so no question, the market got really enthusiastic and sent the stock flying up.

The problem is that the MoU is not a legally binding contract. This is basically an agreement to collaborate and the discussion of some terms of this partnership, but without actually committing. And this is a very important part to understand. The fact that the company announced this memorandum does not mean it will get a cent from Saudis. This is how AI-powered Google search explains what a memorandum of understanding (MoU) is:

A memorandum of understanding (MOU) is a non-binding agreement between two or more parties that outlines a shared understanding of their roles, responsibilities, and objectives in a collaborative project or partnership. It's a formal document that serves to establish a common goal and a framework for cooperation, but it does not create legally enforceable obligations.

After everyone understands what we are dealing with, we can be a little excited about this memorandum. This is basically a handshake between Joby, which is one of the leading companies in the electric air taxi sector, and ALJ, which has a serious infrastructure and transport control across the Gulf region. So the potential is definitely here, but so are the unknowns.

Company Overview

Joby builds electric aircraft that take off and land vertically, which doesn't sound very impressive, because this is what helicopters do, but the technology of Joby is different. This aircraft is designed to be a flying taxi in cities, so it’s allegedly quiet, fast, and with zero emissions. I say “allegedly” only because I didn’t see one and didn’t experience it myself to know for sure. Moreover, I saw some claims that they might not be as quiet and not as nature-friendly as the company claims.

If all of the claims are real, I see a nice potential for this technology. I am not sure how big the market is gonna be, because currently, I could imagine Joby only in 2 cities I've been to - New York and Dubai, and I traveled a bit. These cities are the ones where people use helicopters, so for now, I see it as equivalent.

Now, there’s a reason for this Saudi news to get so much attention. Joby has been building and working on this technology for years. They want to create something big, and finally, we can say we see some progress. Besides, they’re passing the FAA. This is a certification that an aircraft is safe to use and to fly. Joby is currently at the end of the fourth stage of the aircraft design process, and it looks like they will be finishing soon. This could be a positive catalyst for the stock price, as this certificate is almost like FDA approval of a new drug.

They’ve also crossed a huge technical milestone. In April, they began flying full-scale aircraft with a pilot onboard through full transitions, from vertical takeoff into forward flight and back. According to Joby’s CEO JoeBen Bevirt, everything went exactly as they hoped it would:

The aircraft flew exactly as we expected with excellent handling qualities and low pilot workload. Since Buddy made the first transition to wingborne flight, two other Joby pilots have done the same and importantly, pilot-on-board transition flight has now become a routine part of our flight testing and we’re the first eVTOL company to be able to say that.

Joby is getting ready to bring that aircraft to Dubai for testing, and they plan to launch commercial operations also in Dubai by early 2026. If it happens, Joby will beat most of its eVTOL competitors, which are only in early development and testing stages.

The only problem in this bullish story is that the company hasn’t made any sales yet. Joby’s market cap is almost $7 billion. For the company that never earned anything. This is wild.

A Billion-Dollar Opportunity… or is it?

I want to discuss this Saudi deal more closely, but not as a guarantee that the company will start earning. This is a possibility that the company will start selling. Abdul Latif Jameel is one of the biggest conglomerates in the Middle East, and they have business in various industries, from Toyota car dealerships to renewable energy. Partnering with them is indeed a huge deal for the company because it opens the door to a whole region that is ready to throw millions into being seen as futuristic and innovative. I am from Israel, and our country is investing less in it, but Saudi Arabia and the UAE want their country to be the future, and they are succeeding in it. It may not look like it just yet, but believe me when I say, Dubai is transforming every two to three years to be more and more modern and technological. And of course, Joby’s taxi, which flies at a speed of over 200mph and has no emissions, aligns perfectly with this goal.

Management was excited long before the deal. On the Q1 earnings call, Joby’s Executive Chairman Paul Sciarra said they’re having “all the right conversations” about selling aircraft outside the U.S. and that their focus is on “credible customers and pre-orders that are as firm as possible.” I am curious if they meant this specific partnership or some other ones.

Anyway, the direction looks right and the future sounds promising. The company had different types of partnerships before, like Uber (UBER), Delta (DAL), and Toyota (TM), but no meaningful revenue has come from them. At least not until now, and investors had hoped it wouldn’t take so long.

Conclusion

Joby is one of those companies that trade on dreams about the future and promises that may or may not be fulfilled. As I already mentioned, it’s a $7 billion company with zero revenue. I don’t see any reason to look at any of the metrics we usually discuss, like the valuation, the profitability, or the growth. The company doesn’t have anything. This is not even a business yet, at least from my perspective. It’s a very impressive development that may or may not make some money in the future.

This technology has the potential to become truly revolutionary and to change the world. It also has a chance to become a more widely used helicopter in huge cities. As I said before, I see Joby’s taxis on Manhattan, for example. It would improve the city greatly, because the noise from all the copters is unbearable. Will or not this aircraft go further than that? Not for sure. And before you write me comments, I know that the market is supposed to become about $1 billion at some point. I know that Joby is cheaper, and not only the crowd that uses helicopters is supposed to use them.

But the key word in this is “supposed.” I am not playing a fortune teller. If you are fascinated by the idea, go ahead and buy the stock. The company has the money to sustain itself for about two years, and recently, we got some news about a major Toyota investment, which is also a big plus. If you are considering this stock, I would even think about buying a few similar companies just to have them. This is speculative, for sure. Not for value investors, for people who like the risk. The downside here is only 100%, but the upside could be seriously rewarding if it works.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
